text: We present a case of robotic-assisted ventral mesh rectopexy with cholecystectomy in a case of anterior rectocele with cholelithiasis. Patient is a sixty-one year old female with dyspeptic symptoms and gallstone disease diagnosed two years back. She also gives history of difficulty in passing stool which needs splinting of vagina to assist in defecation. There were no associated urinary symptoms. On examination, she had a large anterior rectocele. Her colonoscopy was normal. MRI defecography showed a significant anterior rectocele with minimal descent in the middle and anterior compartment while straining. She was counseled and taken up for robotic ventral mesh rectopexy with cholecystectomy.

In the MR defecography picture shown here, we can appreciate the PIVOC oxygen line, the H line and the M line. The significant amount of anterior rectocele can be well appreciated. The elongated H line measuring eight centimeters and M line measuring six centimeters can be seen. These were our port position for both the procedure with initial patient card being docked near the left hip of the patient. For cholecystectomy redocking is done near the right shoulder.

The uterus is suspended with a polyamide suture, and the appendices epiploica of the sigmoid colon are sutured with a rubber band for traction. The small bowel is positioned on the right side with cotton gauze. A hockey stick incision is marked over the pelvic peritoneum and deepened to expose the sacral promontory. The peritoneal incision is extended along the marked line. The rectovaginal pouch is elevated, and the septum is divided, separating the vaginal vault from the underlying rectum, with both structures visible.

The surgeon dissects down the plane of fibrosis in the area of the rectocele, using traction on the posterior vaginal wall and depressing the rectum with a robotic grasper. A speculum elevates the vagina to aid dissection in the narrow pelvis. The dissection continues until the perineal body and pelvic floor are visible. A 15x4 cm macroporous partially absorbable mesh is fixed to the ventral surface of the mobilized rectum with multiple seromuscular non-absorbable polyester sutures, and the upper end is secured to the sacral promontory. A 2-0 absorbable barbed suture re-approximates the peritoneum and extra-peritonealizes the mesh.

The surgical team redocks the robotic system to proceed with the cholecystectomy. The gallbladder is retracted using the third robotic arm. Adhesions around the gallbladder are released, and the Calot's triangle is dissected to achieve the critical view of safety. The cystic duct and artery are clipped and divided. The gallbladder is carefully dissected from the fossa, ensuring the cystic plate remains intact. The gallbladder specimen is then placed in a retrieval bag and extracted from the surgical site.
Post-operative course was uneventful and the patient was started on oral liquids within six hours and was discharged on the first post-operative day.
